explanation: The natural language states that when leak compensation is enabled, activation of leak compensation shall be triggered by the MinPEEP alarm. This means that if leak compensation is enabled, then whenever the MinPEEP alarm becomes active, leak compensation must be activated. To capture this in LTL, we look for all times when leak compensation is enabled and MinPEEPAlarm is active, and require that leakCompensationActive is true at those times. The formula G(enableLeakCompensation -> (MinPEEPAlarm -> leakCompensationActive)) globally ensures that if leak compensation is enabled and the MinPEEP alarm is active, then leak compensation must be active. Previous LTL properties that might use just G(MinPEEPAlarm -> leakCompensationActive) would not be correct, since it must only apply when leak compensation is enabled.

The natural language requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled, its activation is controlled (i.e., it happens) by the MinPEEP alarm. This implies that leak compensation can only be active if it has been enabled and if the MinPEEP alarm is presently active. In Linear Temporal Logic, this can be captured by requiring that globally (always), whenever leak compensation is both enabled (enableLeakCompensation) and the MinPEEP alarm is active (MinPEEPAlarm), the leak compensation feature shall be active (leakCompensationActive). This is expressed as: G((enableLeakCompensation & MinPEEPAlarm) -> leakCompensationActive).

The natural language requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled by the user, activation of leak compensation shall be triggered or caused by the MinPEEP alarm (SAV.15). There are two relevant atomic propositions: 'enableLeakCompensation' (user enables leak compensation) and 'leakCompensationActive' (leak compensation is active), and 'MinPEEPAlarm' (MinPEEP alarm is active). The property can be captured by stating that, globally, whenever leak compensation is enabled, it can only become active if the MinPEEP alarm is also active (i.e., activation is triggered by the MinPEEP alarm), and leakCompensationActive does not become true until MinPEEPAlarm is true. This is best expressed using the implication and 'until' operator. In particular, 'G(enableLeakCompensation -> (!leakCompensationActive U MinPEEPAlarm))' ensures that after enablement, leakCompensationActive cannot become true before MinPEEPAlarm does. This prevents premature activation and expresses the dependency described. This is more faithful than expressing a simple implication like 'G(MinPEEPAlarm -> leakCompensationActive)', because that would not require enablement, and would not prevent leak compensation being active for unrelated reasons.

The requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled, the leak compensation mechanism shall become active if the MinPEEP alarm is triggered. In terms of temporal logic, this implies that whenever 'enableLeakCompensation' holds, then whenever 'MinPEEPAlarm' becomes true, 'leakCompensationActive' must become true as well. In LTL, we capture this as: globally, if leak compensation is enabled, then it is always the case that if the MinPEEP alarm is active, leak compensation must also be active. This property can be written as G(enableLeakCompensation -> G(MinPEEPAlarm -> leakCompensationActive)). This ensures that after leak compensation is enabled, every time the MinPEEP alarm is triggered, leak compensation is (or becomes) active. This satisfies the causal dependency described in the requirement.

The requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled, its activation shall be triggered by the MinPEEP alarm. This means that whenever leak compensation is enabled, it should only become active in response to the MinPEEP alarm, and not otherwise. Therefore, in temporal logic, this can be stated as: globally, if leak compensation is enabled, then it will only become active if the MinPEEP alarm is also active (i.e., being enabled is a precondition, and actual activation must be synchronized with the MinPEEP alarm event). Since LTL cannot encode causality or data-passing, the most direct encoding is: globally, whenever leakCompensationActive is true, it must be the case that enableLeakCompensation and MinPEEPAlarm are both true (since only MinPEEPAlarm may trigger activation when enabled).

The natural language requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled, it shall only become active if triggered by the MinPEEP alarm. To model this, we can use: G(enableLeakCompensation -> (leakCompensationActive -> MinPEEPAlarm)). This LTL means that, at all times, if leak compensation is enabled, then whenever leak compensation is active, the MinPEEP alarm must also be active. This property prevents leak compensation from becoming active in enabled mode unless the MinPEEP alarm is concurrently active, matching the 'shall be activated by' logic in the requirement. Other LTLs might only require that MinPEEP alarm and leak compensation active occur at the same time, or ignore the dependency on enablement, but this LTL correctly ties the activation condition to both enablement and alarm status.

The natural language requirement states that whenever leak compensation is enabled, it should only become active when the MinPEEP alarm is triggered. In LTL, this is best translated as: 'whenever enableLeakCompensation is true, then leakCompensationActive can only become true if MinPEEPAlarm is true.' In other words, at all times, it is not allowed for leak compensation to be active in any state unless both it is enabled and the MinPEEP alarm is active. The LTL formula G(leakCompensationActive -> (enableLeakCompensation & MinPEEPAlarm)) succinctly captures this by stating: globally, if leak compensation is active, then it implies (1) it is enabled and (2) the MinPEEP alarm is active. This ensures that leak compensation activation is strictly governed by both the enabling and the MinPEEP alarm signal, satisfying the original requirement. 

Alternative weaker candidates (not produced here) might incorrectly allow leakCompensationActive to become true as soon as enableLeakCompensation is true, regardless of MinPEEPAlarm, or might not enforce continuous causality, which would not capture the requirement that the MinPEEP alarm is the activator when enabled.

The requirement states: 'When enabled, the leak compensation shall be activated by the MinPEEP alarm (SAV.15)'. The intention is that when leak compensation is enabled by the user, its activation (i.e., leakCompensationActive) is triggered whenever the MinPEEP alarm is active. To model this in LTL, we want to express that globally, whenever leak compensation is enabled and the MinPEEP alarm is active, this leads to leak compensation being active. That is, at all times, if enableLeakCompensation and MinPEEPAlarm are both true, then eventually (possibly immediately), leakCompensationActive becomes true. This can be captured by the LTL property: G((enableLeakCompensation & MinPEEPAlarm) -> F leakCompensationActive). This ensures that whenever leak compensation is enabled and the MinPEEP alarm is raised, leak compensation activation necessarily follows (within the future).

The requirement states that when leak compensation is enabled, its activation should be triggered by the MinPEEP alarm. In LTL, this is best captured by saying: Always, if leak compensation is enabled (enableLeakCompensation), then whenever MinPEEP alarm becomes active (MinPEEPAlarm), leak compensation must (eventually, possibly immediately) become active (leakCompensationActive). This ensures that enabling this feature causes it to respond (activate) when the required alarm triggers. No other atomic propositions are relevant, and we cannot use numerical comparisons or refer to SAV.15 except for the association with MinPEEPAlarm. Thus, the LTL captures the business logic that leak compensation, once enabled, is required to activate in response to the MinPEEP alarm.

The requirement specifies that whenever the system is 'enabled for leak compensation' (using 'enableLeakCompensation'), the leak compensation will be activated by the MinPEEP alarm. In LTL, this translates to: globally, whenever 'enableLeakCompensation' becomes true, if the 'MinPEEPAlarm' is true, then 'leakCompensationActive' must also be true. This is captured by G((enableLeakCompensation & MinPEEPAlarm) -> leakCompensationActive), expressing that if leak compensation is enabled and the MinPEEP alarm is active, then leak compensation is activated. No previous LTL property was presented, so this directly answers the natural language requirement.
